role_summary: SNCC field secretary in Lowndes County, Alabama (1965-1968), where she designed the comic books and billboards that supported the Lowndes County Freedom Democratic Party campaign (the all-Black party whose ballot symbol was a black panther, the iconography that named the Black Panther Party in Oakland the following year); first Black chief programming executive at the Public Broadcasting Service (PBS, 1989-1995), the senior-most African-American programmer at a major U.S. television network at that date; founder of Magic Box Mediaworks (1995).
ai_reading: Lawson's neighbor counts are moderate (peak 85), with Charles McLaurin (entry 17) as the top match and the broader cluster including Dr. Doris Derby (entry 32), Gwendolyn M. Patton (entry 52, who also worked Tuskegee), and H. Jack Geiger (entry 54). The Patton pairing is the catalog's clearest Tuskegee-women-organizers cosine; both moved through the same campus and the same mid-1960s Alabama-organizing circuit. The novel observation her page surfaces is the iconographer-as-organizer signature: Lawson's Lowndes County work created the black-panther ballot symbol that, after the Lowndes County Freedom Democratic Party's 1966 campaigns, became the visual identifier of the Oakland Black Panther Party founded that October by Newton and Seale. The catalog's BPP-founder pages (Aaron Dixon, Elmer Dixon, Kathleen Cleaver, Elbert Howard) cluster on the Nonviolence-as-Theology pole; Lawson's Lowndes County work originated the iconography their party adopted, an iconographic-to-organizational transit that runs through her embedding signature.
biographical_paragraph: While Lawson's LoC oral history carries a not-auditable audit tier (the catalog cannot independently certify the transcript against primary sources for this entry, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external archives before citation), her external biographical record is well-documented [src: 1, src: 2]. Jennifer Karen Lawson was born June 8, 1946 in Fairfield, Alabama. She first became active in the civil rights movement as a Fairfield High School student in 1963, and continued at Tuskegee University before leaving school in 1965 to work full-time as a SNCC field secretary [src: 2]. She organized in Lowndes County, Alabama (the Alabama Black Belt county SNCC and the Lowndes County Freedom Democratic Party were building), and designed comic books and billboards in support of the LCFO campaigns; the LCFO chose a black panther as its ballot symbol, the iconography Huey P. Newton and Bobby Seale adopted later that year when they founded the Black Panther Party for Self-Defense in Oakland in October 1966 [src: 2]. In 1989 PBS hired her as the network's first chief programming executive, the highest-ranking Black woman in public television at that date; during her PBS tenure (1989-1995) she developed the scheduling and promotion strategies for Ken Burns's The Civil War (1990) and Baseball (1994), the network's most-watched documentary series [src: 2]. She founded Magic Box Mediaworks in 1995 and continued producing documentary films, including an eight-hour series on Africa with National Geographic [src: 2].
movement_context: The Lowndes County, Alabama organizing project Lawson moved into in 1965 was the SNCC field operation Stokely Carmichael directed across 1965-1966 in the Alabama Black Belt county whose population was roughly 80 percent Black yet whose voter rolls in March 1965 listed zero registered Black voters; the project's structural objective was to register the county's eligible Black majority and to build an independent Black political party that could contest the November 1966 county elections under its own ballot line rather than through the white-controlled state Democratic Party [src: 4]. The Lowndes County Freedom Organization (LCFO) the project produced was the first independent Black political party in the post-Reconstruction South; the organizing model was the Alabama statute (Title 17) that allowed any group winning a county-level political party convention to place its slate on the ballot, the legal route the SNCC field staff and the local farmers' and freedom-house network spent the year between January 1965 and the November 1966 election walking together [src: 4]. The black-panther ballot symbol Lawson and Courtland Cox (catalog entry 24) co-produced for the LCFO political-education materials, the symbol the Alabama Title 17 statute required every party to register so the county's many non-literate voters could identify the ballot line, was the visual identity the Oakland-based Black Panther Party for Self-Defense founded by Huey P. Newton and Bobby Seale on October 15, 1966 took its name and iconography from; the inter-organizational lineage from the Lowndes County Freedom Organization through the Oakland Black Panther Party is the institutional history Hasan Kwame Jeffries documents in Bloody Lowndes (New York University Press 2009) [src: 4]. The graphic-design work Lawson did for the LCFO sits inside a broader SNCC visual-identity tradition that also produced Doug Harris's photographic record of the LCFO campaign and the design work Dorothy Foreman Zellner (catalog entry 30) produced at SNCC's request for the Oakland Black Panther Party in 1966-1967 [src: 5]. Lawson's transition from SNCC field organizing through the 1970s into documentary-film production placed her in the Black-documentary institutional cohort that organized the National Black Programming Consortium (NBPC) in 1979, the membership organization founded to address the underrepresentation of Black-produced programming on public television and to build a parallel production pipeline outside the major-network commissioning system [src: 6]. Her account of the Lowndes County years, including the comic-book and billboard work, appears in the 2010 University of Illinois Press collective memoir Hands on the Freedom Plow: Personal Accounts by Women in SNCC, the principal collective archival document of the SNCC women's cohort [src: 7].
legacy_and_reception: Lawson's 1989 hire by the Public Broadcasting Service made her the network's first Chief Programming Executive and at that date the senior-most African-American programmer at any major U.S. television network; her PBS tenure (1989-1995) coincided with the first six annual seasons of the POV (Point of View) independent-documentary series (launched 1988) and with the launch of the P.O.V. Interactive web project, the institutional anchor for the network's documentary programming through the 1990s [src: 8]. The PBS programs commissioned and scheduled during her tenure included Ken Burns's The Civil War (1990) and Baseball (1994), the network's most-watched documentary series, and the multipart series on African American history and culture that built the prime-time documentary slate she had been hired to develop [src: 8]. After her PBS tenure she joined the Ford Foundation as a senior program officer (1995), founded Magic Box Mediaworks as an independent production company (1995), and from 2005 to 2012 served as Chief Operating Officer and General Manager of WHUT-TV (the Howard University-licensed PBS member station in Washington, D.C., the first Black-owned and licensed public-television station in the United States) [src: 8, src: 2]. Her 1979 co-founding of the National Black Programming Consortium (the membership organization rebranded as Black Public Media in 2014) is the institutional anchor for the continuing pipeline of Black-produced public-television programming; she received the Black Public Media Trailblazer Award in 2019 in recognition of the institutional history her work traces [src: 6].

gallery.caption: Lowndes County Freedom Organization (LCFO) political-education flyer, 1966. The Wikimedia Commons file page attributes the flyer to SNCC Political Education Materials produced by Courtland Cox and Jennifer Lawson; the black-panther ballot symbol Lawson drew for the LCFO became the visual identity the Oakland-based Black Panther Party for Self-Defense founded by Huey P. Newton and Bobby Seale on October 15, 1966 took its name and graphic identity from. Digitized by the Freedom Archives on September 21, 2015.

gallery.alt: Stokely Carmichael at a 1966 Mississippi press conference
gallery.caption: Stokely Carmichael (later Kwame Ture) at a 1966 Mississippi press conference, the same year SNCC's Lowndes County, Alabama field operation Carmichael directed produced the November 1966 Lowndes County Freedom Organization ballot campaign that Lawson's comic-book and billboard work supported. The image is in the public domain in the United States as a work published before 1978 without copyright notice.
